title: Vomit on Silk — Remove Solids First, Then Navigate the Acid Minefield
stainChemistry: Vomit is a multi-component stain: (1) hydrochloric acid from stomach (pH 1.5–3.5) — immediately attacks silk's protein fibroin; (2) partially digested food proteins — hydrogen-bond to silk fiber; (3) bile pigments (bilirubin/biliverdin) — produce yellow-green chromophore that is extremely difficult to remove; (4) food dyes, fats, and tannins depending on stomach contents. On silk, vomit's stomach acid is the primary threat — silk tolerates pH 4.0–7.0, and vomit's pH below 3.5 can weaken silk fibroin on contact. The combination of acid damage + protein deposit + bile pigment makes this one of the hardest protein stains on silk.
whyThisWorks: Speed is everything. Removing solids and diluting the stomach acid with cold water within minutes prevents acid damage to silk fibroin. The neutral-to-slightly-alkaline protein formula breaks the food protein bonds, but must be carefully controlled on silk (pH ceiling of 8, short dwell). Bile pigment (the yellow-green color that persists after protein removal) is the hardest component — it requires cautious oxidative treatment with dilute peroxide, tested first because silk's tolerance is limited. The entire protocol avoids enzymes (silk is protein) and aggressive chemistry.

spottingProtocol.agent: Mechanical removal — scrape off solids IMMEDIATELY
spottingProtocol.technique: Scrape with dull edge (spoon, bone spatula) toward center
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Do this FIRST — before any liquid
spottingProtocol.instruction: Remove solid vomit material immediately using the back of a spoon or dull spatula edge. Work from the outside edges toward the center to avoid spreading. On silk, be extremely gentle — silk's smooth fibroin surface chafes easily. Collect solids on a paper towel. Do NOT push solids into the fabric. Every second counts here — stomach acid is actively attacking the silk fiber.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back of fabric using water gun at safe distance (6+ inches)
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold — below 80°F. Cold water dilutes stomach acid and dissolves water-soluble components.
spottingProtocol.instruction: Immediately flush with cold water from the back. This serves two critical purposes: (1) dilutes and flushes out stomach acid before it degrades silk fibroin; (2) removes water-soluble vomit components (urea, salts, sugars). Use very gentle pressure on silk — hold water gun 6+ inches away. Place silk face-down on clean white towel and watch for color transfer. Keep flushing until transfer is minimal.

spottingProtocol.technique: Apply with soft sponge, gentle press — no brush on silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply NSD with soft sponge or cotton pad. Light pressing action only — never brush silk. The neutral pH surfactant emulsifies the fat and food components without stressing silk. NSD is pH-neutral, which is ideal for silk's safe range. Work gently from outside in.

spottingProtocol.technique: Apply with dropper, light press with cotton pad
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 2 minutes maximum — shorter dwell than blood because vomit has already acid-stressed the silk
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply diluted protein formula for the food protein component. On vomit + silk, use a MORE dilute ratio (1:2) than for blood (1:1) because the silk has already been stressed by stomach acid. Keep dwell to 2 minutes maximum. The alkali breaks protein hydrogen bonds to silk fibroin. Watch for dye change — flush immediately if color shifts.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold water flush + acetic acid neutralization
spottingProtocol.technique: Flush thoroughly, then apply dilute acetic acid
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush all alkaline chemistry out completely. Then apply dilute acetic acid (28% GAA diluted 1:20) to return silk to safe acidic pH. This is doubly important on vomit-stained silk: the fiber has been stressed by both stomach acid AND your alkaline protein formula. Returning to safe pH quickly minimizes cumulative damage.

spottingProtocol.agent: Hydrogen peroxide 3% (ONLY if yellow-green bile pigment persists, and ONLY after testing)
spottingProtocol.technique: Test hidden area first, then apply briefly to bile stain
spottingProtocol.instruction: Bile pigment (bilirubin/biliverdin) produces a persistent yellow-green color that protein formula alone won't remove. If this persists, test 3% peroxide on a hidden silk area for 60 seconds. If no color change or fiber damage, apply to the bile stain for 60 seconds maximum. Flush immediately with cold water. Silk has very limited peroxide tolerance — do NOT exceed 60 seconds. If bile stain persists after one peroxide application, DO NOT repeat — escalate to professional.

spottingProtocol.agent: Final cold flush + lay flat to dry
spottingProtocol.technique: Cold flush, reshape, dry flat on towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final cold water flush. Lay silk flat on clean dry towel — never hang wet silk. Do not use heat. Inspect when dry. If bile stain persists, this needs professional attention — bile pigment on silk is extremely difficult and the margin for error is too small for further attempts.

homeSolutions: FRESH (act within 5 minutes): Scrape off solids with a spoon — gently on silk. Immediately flush the back of the fabric with cold running water to dilute and remove stomach acid. Blot with clean white cloth dampened with cold water. Apply a TINY amount of pH-neutral soap (Dawn Free & Clear) diluted in cold water. Press gently with cloth. Rinse cold. Lay flat to dry.

DRIED/SET: Gently scrape off dried residue. Sponge with cold water to rehydrate. Apply a small amount of Dawn Free & Clear in cold water, press gently. Rinse. Do NOT use enzyme detergent on silk. If a yellow-green bile stain remains, this is beyond safe home treatment on silk — take to a professional.

WHEN TO STOP: On silk, stop after one round of gentle soap and cold water. If any color remains — especially yellow-green from bile — do NOT attempt bleaching, peroxide, or ammonia at home on silk. The risk of compounding damage to an already acid-stressed fiber is too high. Professional treatment is the right call.
materialWarnings: NEVER hot water on vomit + silk — heat coagulates food proteins permanently into the fiber. Cold only.
NEVER protease enzymes on silk — silk IS a protein fiber. Enzymes digest the silk along with the food protein in the vomit.
NEVER chlorine bleach on silk — destroys protein structure and causes irreversible yellowing.
NEVER brush silk — chafes the smooth fibroin surface, creating visible damage that can't be repaired.
NEVER leave vomit on silk — stomach acid (pH 1.5–3.5) actively weakens silk fibroin. Speed of treatment directly impacts fiber survival.
NEVER use full-strength protein formula on vomit-stained silk — the fiber is already acid-stressed. Dilute 1:2 minimum, dwell 2 minutes max.

professional.name: Street's Protein Formula
professional.use: Food protein component — dilute 1:2 for silk, 2-minute dwell max
professional.note: Use MORE dilute than for blood on silk. Vomit's stomach acid has already weakened the silk — aggressive chemistry compounds the damage. Test dye first.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
professional.use: Bile pigment (yellow-green) — last resort, 60 seconds max on silk
professional.note: Test hidden area first. One application only. If bile persists after one attempt, professional reducing bleach may be needed.

professional.name: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent)
professional.use: Primary cleaner — safe pH-neutral surfactant for silk
professional.note: The safest wet-side agent for silk. pH neutral, gentle emulsification. Use as your primary cleaner before escalating to protein formula.

consumer.name: Dawn Free & Clear Dish Soap
consumer.use: Mild surfactant for cold-water treatment — enzyme-free, dye-free
consumer.note: A few drops in cold water. The safest consumer product for silk. No enzymes, no dyes, no fragrance. Press gently — never rub.

consumer.name: White Distilled Vinegar
consumer.use: Final acid rinse to restore silk to safe pH range after any treatment
consumer.note: 1 tablespoon per cup of cold water as final rinse. Protects silk from residual alkaline damage.

escalation.when: Yellow-green bile pigment persists after protein treatment, the silk shows signs of acid damage (rough or weakened areas where vomit sat), the vomit was not treated quickly and sat on silk for hours, or the silk is a valuable garment (evening wear, bridal, couture).
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner: 'This is vomit on silk. I removed solids and flushed with cold water immediately. The protein stain is mostly out but there's a yellow-green shadow from bile pigment. The silk was exposed to stomach acid for approximately [X minutes/hours]. I did NOT use enzymes, bleach, or hot water. Please assess the silk's condition before applying any aggressive chemistry — the fiber may be acid-weakened.'
escalation.specialistType: Dry cleaner experienced with silk and bile stain removal — ideally one with wet cleaning capability and access to professional reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) for bile pigment. If the silk shows physical damage (weakened areas), a textile restoration specialist may be needed.

solventNote: For dry cleaners: Pre-spot vomit on silk BEFORE machine cleaning. The protein component sets at drycleaning temperatures. Cold protein formula (diluted for silk), flush, acetic acid neutralize. Hydrocarbon is preferred over PERC for silk (less odor retention, better hand-feel). GreenEarth is ideal for luxury silk. For bile pigment residue: on the post-spot board, consider a brief application of reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite at 1g/kg for silk — very limited, brief contact, professional use only). Reducing bleach decolorizes bilirubin without the oxidative stress of peroxide.
